---
title: "Build a design matrix"
subtitle: "Model event timing with HRFs and inspect the generated regressors."
jupyter: python3
---

## Create Events

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import fmrimod as fm
from fmrimod.design_colmap import design_colmap

events = pd.DataFrame({
    "onset": [0, 8, 16, 24, 32, 40],
    "condition": ["face", "scene", "face", "scene", "face", "scene"],
    "response_time": [0.8, 1.1, 0.7, 0.9, 1.0, 1.2],
})
events
```

## Define the Acquisition Grid

```{python}
sf = fm.SamplingFrame(blocklens=60, TR=1.0)
sf.samples[:8]
```

## Build the Model

The formula below creates condition-specific canonical HRF regressors. The
result is an event model with a concrete design matrix and column metadata.

```{python}
model = fm.event_model(
    'onset ~ hrf(condition, basis="spmg1")',
    data=events,
    sampling_frame=sf,
)

model.design_matrix.shape
```

```{python}
model.column_names
```

## Inspect Column Metadata

```{python}
design_colmap(model)[["col", "name", "condition", "role", "basis_name"]]
```

## Add a Parametric Modulator

To model a condition modulated by a trial-by-trial continuous variable
(reaction time, accuracy, confidence, ...), use the typed-spec
`modulators=` keyword. This expands the term into an unmodulated
boxcar plus one regressor per modulator. By default each modulator
is mean-centered at the raw-value level (pre-convolution), giving
the unmodulated boxcar and modulated columns the near-orthogonal
structure that makes per-modulator betas interpretable.

```{python}
from fmrimod.spec import hrf

modulated_spec = hrf("condition", modulators=("response_time",))
modulated = fm.event_model(modulated_spec, data=events, sampling_frame=sf)

modulated.design_matrix.shape
```

See the [parametric modulators](parametric-modulators.qmd) tutorial
for the full story on centering semantics, the `center_modulators=`
opt-out, and why fmrimod does not orthogonalize modulators
automatically.
